speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gabbai, you were just telling us that on the march it was raining and you were eating mud. Can I ask you, while you were marching, did you see any of the general Polish population? Did you see people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was on-- already in Austria, I think, when-- when I was-- it was day. And there were a lot of people, but nobody was willing to do anything, you know. They were looking at us. Probably they-- they knew what was going on, really. But nobody came-- came to help in any way. The only thing that I remember is when we were in the wagons and we were passing by, by Czechoslovakia, you know, in the town of Brno-- B-R-N-O-- you know, in the station, I think there were some people, you know, trying to give us some food. That's the only-- the only one I know in Czechoslovakia. But the other ones in Austria, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, while you were on the open wagons, about how long would you say you traveled on these trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, maybe, overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die around you while you were on the wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah, I don't remember then.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the train stopped, where were you and what happened? Well, [? with the sister, ?] we were in the-- you know, the town of Mauthausen, which was the Danube River town. And we-- we started again with the same thing, like being in Auschwitz. You got a selection time you go through. You take a-- they give you a cold and cold shower, and then if you have any hair, they cut your hair. And then they assign you. I was there very little-- you know, maybe, I don't know, a day or two if I was there. And then they assigned me to go to Melk. I was not too far away from there. And that was for the IG Farben. And the-- the-- my work consisted-- it was very, very cold. And the only thing-- the work consisted of just throwing a lot of sand on a conveyor belt there. And that-- I-- I was there until April 1945. And that means from January-- yeah, quite a few months there. And while you were in Melk, what were the conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad-- every condition is-- you know, we weren't used to such a cold weather-- very, very cold weather. And the only thing we survive is because working at the Sonderkommando, I was well nourished for the march. And until there, you know, I was surviving, you know, even with very, very little food. That came about to the end of it when from Melk, I went to Ebensee, Austria, you know, in April 1945. Then in Ebensee, the work there was in-- in tunnels of-- in caves of mountains, moving stones of hundred, 200 pounds from one place to another. The only thing they were waiting is to kill us. They were-- we heard the rumors that they want to put us all in the caves, and then put a bomb and finish with us. But they didn't succeeded, you know. But-- and it happens that one morning, you know, I just-- I get up, 5:30 or 6 o'clock in the morning, and there is such a silence-- silence, nothing, nobody. Just-- just heard the birds singing, the only thing. You know, Ebensee is a beautiful tourist place. It's one of the most beautiful places in Austria. And seeing around nobody, I come out-- no guards, nothing. So we come out. You know, there was nobody in the camp anymore. It took a couple of hours and the American army came in. And we start crying, and crying, and crying. And they start you know, taking care of us. And the only problem-- I think they were giving soups. And soups-- it's-- a lot of people had diarrhea and they died. I had the knowledge of find a chicken and some potatoes from I don't know where. I went all around. Because I was 67 pounds at that time. You know, I couldn't resist more than another week. And I used to eat every hour on the hour, a little bit, a little bit. It took me only a few days to have my strength. This is how I started, you know, when the French United Nations relief came in, and they give me a job to be able to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --let's go back a little bit. And from Melk to Ebensee, how did you get to Ebensee? What was the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: In trucks. And there's no reason. Probably they finished the work there. That was-- you know, IG Farben took-- you know, the German army gave them workers. And they finished the job. And they [INAUDIBLE]-- they have to move us someplace else. They moved me to Ebensee, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Ebensee, what did you see? What kind of a camp was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There weren't too many people there-- probably 5,000, 6,000 people were there. And I stay very little there. It was a month, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of condition were these people in that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very bad. People were very, very-- I don't think anybody could survive anymore. It's just so much that your body can take. After a while, you know, it's the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in being in Ebensee, and being so debilitated physically, how are you able to work with those stones? How did you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You know, you get-- you will be surprised how the human mind and body can-- when you have the-- the-- you know, [ITALIAN] they say in Italian, you know, that to live. You know, and that is-- we weren't-- I wasn't giving up. I wasn't giving up. When I left-- when I left Birkenau, every place I went, the first thing they were doing is asking, who work in the Sonderkommando? They didn't want anyone alive from the Sonderkommando. They knew too much. We knew a lot. So we were all with our lips sealed-- nothing. Nobody said anything. The minute you said anything, you are free. You are finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what do you think it was that-- that made you not want to give up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- you know, I had the-- the will to live, to see the world, to see again what was going on. And most of all, I wanted to tell the world. I wasn't talking for many, many years. I wanted to take the world, you know, exactly what happened to the final solution. You know, it was a very big experience for me that I went back now, because I saw the other camps, too, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to discuss that with you, but let's-- let's go back to what we were talking about. So [SIGHS] in Ebensee, on the day of liberation, when you heard that incredible silence, did you know right away what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I know-- I knew that the war was over. I knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when the Americans came in? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- you know, how can you describe? You know, you know, anything-- without anything wanted, you know, the-- you start crying and-- and from joy, from joy. You know, finally, finally, you are liberated. And see what happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do right after you got your strength back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think back, you know, the just-- the-- the director, [? de la Foret, ?] came in from United Nations Relief. I think I was 135. And he took me with him. And he gave me instructions to-- to take 10, 12 Greeks, if they knew about-- if they are butchers, you know, to go around and pick up anything I could take, of meat or anything else, to be able to bring to the camp to survive. And this is what I did. I went down with these people. And I used to take. You know, I went all around. And I didn't ask too many questions. I tell, just clean them up and bring them over. And this is how, for a while, I work with him. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the SS? Did you see them? Did you know what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never-- I never saw any one of them, never saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the kappos that were in charge of you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the kappos that were with the crematorium-- they all died. They all went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Ebensee?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ebensee, I don't even remember if I had a cup? I know I was in a barrack, you know. I-- In the morning, they tell me go on, you know, take me out, take me in the-- in the caves. There was nothing to do. Just they wanted to eliminate us as soon as possible. And it wasn't done. The Jewish people-- always we survive. I don't care what they do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you started working for this relief-- the relief organization, what exactly was the organization called that  you were working for at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The UNNRA-- U-N-N-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked until-- from May 6 to June 21. And then-- and then they-- they selected me and Danny. I was with Danny, you know. I have a story about Danny to tell you. A few days after the liberation, you know, I was walking in the camp and I saw a pile of cadavers. And a murmur, a voice say, ah, ah. I have a curiosity. What-- what, they are all dead. What is that-- that man doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there. And I go close to him. And I see his-- his legs were all full of pus, you know. So I went around and I see it's-- it's my friend Danny. I said, Danny, Danny, Danny. I took it in my arms. I took it in my barracks. And for days and days, with a little bit of fresh meat and everything, I-- I brought him to life. And from then on, the two of us were the first one to leave Ebensee, go to-- also the Venezia is with us. The-- Bruno Venezia, you know, Shlomo, not the other-- not Morris. Morris wasn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went in, I remember, with-- they put us in a truck and going to Bologna. And then, from Bologna-- but Shlomo got sick, was spitting blood, and took him back. And they put-- took him to [? Mirano, ?] to the-- a convalescent home there, where he had a-- he has one lung only. But he is very well. He survive. And we, me and Danny, went to Rome. And from Rome, we are the first Greeks to go by air, by plane, to Athens, Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you return back to Greece?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you returned and you arrived in Athens, what did you find there? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when we were before leaving, they said, don't forget, when you go back you'll be a good life. Lots of money will given to you and everything. Didn't happen. The Greek government took us and says, go. So I had an aunt, my aunt, that was with her husband. And the family went to the Partisans-- came back. They were there. And we notified them and they pick me up. And Danny has his own people there. And they nourish me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you stay in Athens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I stay in Athens from '45 to '51, when I came to United States. I-- you know, I work for few years for the American Joint Distribution Committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the liaison between them and the Greek government, taking out all the food and clothing they were sending to the survivors without paying any duties.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother Jack? Where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Jack-- I didn't know, when I was there, but he showed up about four months afterwards. He showed up. He was in good spirits and everything. And a couple of months later, his wife showed up. She was-- she had, I think, pulmonary dig-- you know, disease, and-- but she got-- she got well. And they both left in 1949. They went to Israel, in a [? moshav, ?] you know, above Kfar Saba, where they live. And they had the two children and grandchildren. And my-- but the wife passed away about five, six years ago. And my brother passed away in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the rest of your family? Did you ever find out exactly what happened to them? And when did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in the crematorium, I knew exactly what happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever return to Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: I returned to Salonika for a couple of days, 45 years later. I didn't recognize anything. I wasn't interested anymore. It was only-- you know, from all the 60,000 Jews that went to Poland, only 1,750 return.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean 1,750 people returned? So you-- you mentioned that in 1951 you came to the United States. What made you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since I was in Greece when the Joint Distribution closed, and I was an Italian citizen living in Greece, and start taking permits-- that was the Truman Doctrine in 1951, where 250,000 displaced persons from-- they could emigrate to the United States. And I was sponsored originally from the Jewish community of Cleveland, Ohio, which they pay my expenses to come over. But I went there for a very short time. And I wanted to be on my own, so I came to California, where the weather is much better for me. I was used to a warm weather.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Cleveland, did you meet any-- anybody there that became part of your life, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I stayed very short time. I-- I met-- when I came to Cleveland, there came a very nice lady to meet me. And I-- I wasn't speaking English at that time, so we spoke French. And he start asking-- telling me that, you know, we give 10 cents for a razor. I says, I want something to work. I am not interested in having, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, well, you know, my-- my husband is a sales manager for the Bloomfield Company, of one of the biggest manufacturers of dresses, you know, just bought a new Cadillac, 1951. And he would like a chauffeur. You know, he's having a hernia surgery now, but he'll be well in a few days. I says, I'm a-- I'm a chauffeur. So and I says, why don't we go to the DMV and get a license. And I can take it in Spanish. So I went there and we got the license right away. And a few days later, he took me and-- he-- in a few words, he told me you have to take me in 22 hours at [? Mulerbach ?] Hotel in Kansas City, Missouri. I said-- in my way, I said, go [NON-ENGLISH], you know, in the back. And I take a card-- you know, a map. And here I'm there in 22 hours, I took him to the [? Mulerbach ?] Hotel in Kansas City, Missouri.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stay there for a while. And first he told me that no, this is too expensive here. You have to go across the street. I says, yeah, I'll go. He was paying me well, at that time giving me $125 a week, you know-- good money. So I-- I said, OK, then I'll come and help you. And the only thing, I was smiling and showing the dresses to all these old ladies from Pasadena, you know, from around Missouri there. You know, I was-- I was good in this work. [LAUGHS] And I knew enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I smile. And he was writing orders. This, And he said to me, you know, move to me-- with me at [LAUGHS] the Mulerbach Hotel as my-- then the Missouri-- the Kansas River was flooding up, you know. And he said to me, I can't use you anymore. I said, OK. I called New York, a friend of mine. I went to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: From then, a friend of mine from California-- we were traveling together, coming to this country, to the USA-- by the name of Nico Minardos. He was studying motion picture directing. And he was nine years younger than I was. He write me a note in New York and says, beautiful girls, sandy beaches, California. I took the train bus, and I came-- I'm here. [LAUGHS] That was the story. And in 1953, I made a picture with-- with 20th Century Fox. At that time, Spyros Skouras was-- wanted to have about 300 Greeks and select eight of them for a movie. In 1953, it was the Korean War-- a Greek battalion fight in Korea. So one of the eight, I was the one. I work with Victor Mature, Alexander Scourby, and Lee Marvin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the film? Why did you end your acting career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah? Because at that time, I saw most of the actors were in the unemployment lines. And I said that in this country, you have to start from the bottom, not from the top. So I went one day that-- and I made application for Lancer Fabrics and I start sweeping the floor and a year later became a director of one of the companies, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1953. And I was married for 24 years. Then we got separated. And we have a daughter which finish Berkeley University and-- but unfortunately for 11 years she's with [? fibrositis ?] or fibromyalgia. It's a disorder of the-- disorder of the muscles. There is no cure. And two, three years ago, she fell down from a balcony and she broke her feet and her back. And unfortunately, she's in a wheelchair for now, for a long, long time. So my ex-wife takes care of them and I take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- what-- can you tell us her names please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is Rhoda Allegra Gabbai.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 19-- 15 of December 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is she named after?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's named after her grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your former wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My former wife's name is Dana Rosalie Gabbai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, what do you do with your days? What do you do with your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I-- most of my time, I still-- now that I'm retired, I still go and keep my acquaintances with the business I was in. And two, three hours, from 12:00 to 3:00 in the afternoon, I always go to a-- I belong to a spa for many, many, many years. I do exercise for 40 years. And I try to have a quality life, not quantity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, in all these years since the war, have you suffered with nightmares and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first 10 years were-- were terrible nightmares, you know, practically daily or weekly, you know. And my ex-wife was very good for me. You know, she's-- she took care of me very-- very nicely. But still from time to time-- and now, once we are talking, I was in  Poland about the 12th of October to the-- to the 20-- to the 19. I was in Poland. I went to Auschwitz and Birkenau. And I had 22-- the director of the Aish HaTorah from London-- he is the son of a good friend of mine. And he asked me if I will accompany them, the 22, 24 people-- mostly girls and boys, 20 to 30 years old, from the University of London-- to-- they were-- they were wanting to have a lot of knowledge, and want to have somebody that was there, and especially that I was in the Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to all the camps. And when we arrived Birkenau, I went to crematorium 2, which we destroy half of it when we were there. And I found it exactly the same way, after 52 years. Majdanek is intact, is just what it was before when-- when they started. And I was very surprised to see Treblinka in the forest where very few-- they tell me that very few Germans knew where Treblinka was, because whoever went there could never come back from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only-- the only thing, there are stones from all the Jewish communities from the world for the Jews that perished there. It was quite a trip for me. And these young Jewish people-- they were so caring and loving that I can't [? calm ?] [? down. ?] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did these young people ask you? What kind of things were you able to share with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I share all my experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you now that you've done it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think something came out of my-- my soul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's almost 52 years since your liberation and since you were there, is there any kind of a message that you'd like to give for future generations, anything that you'd like to say to anyone who might be watching this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that history doesn't repeat itself. And always to be strong. And nowadays, you know, there is the state of Israel. We'll be able-- you know, any Jew that wants to go and be there, to be safe and sound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that I may have forgotten to talk to you about, or anything you may have wanted to say that perhaps you thought of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I think we covered, pretty good, you know, the story. I'm happy to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I really want to thank you, so much, for taking the time to share your testimony with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
